```java
 2: s += "t";
 3: } catch(Exception e) {

11: straight to the finally block. Then the code after the try statement is run. We know; this
12: is a silly example. Expect to see examples like this on the OCA exam.
13: System.exit
14: There is one exception to “the finally block always runs after the catch block” rule:
15: Java deﬁnes a method that you call as System.exit(0);. The integer parameter is the
16: error code that gets returned. System.exit tells Java, “Stop. End the program right now.
17: Do not pass go. Do not collect $200.” When System.exit is called in the try or catch
18: block, finally does not run.
19: Catching Various Types of Exceptions
20: So far, you have been catching only one type of exception. Now let’s see what happens
21: when different types of exceptions can be thrown from the same method.
22: Creating your own exceptions is not on the OCA exam, but it is on the OCP exam.
23: However, the OCA exam can defi ne basic exceptions to show you the hierarchy. You only
24: need to do two things with this information. First, you must be able to recognize if the
25: exception is a checked or an unchecked exception. Second, you need to determine if any of
26: the exceptions are subclasses of the others.
27: class AnimalsOutForAWalk extends RuntimeException { }
28: class ExhibitClosed extends RuntimeException { }
29: class ExhibitClosedForLunch extends ExhibitClosed { }
30: In this example, there are three custom exceptions. All are unchecked exceptions
31: because they directly or indirectly extend RuntimeException. Now we catch both types of
32: exceptions and handle them by printing out the appropriate message:
33: public void visitPorcupine() {
34: try {
35: seeAnimal();
36: } catch (AnimalsOutForAWalk e) {// first catch block
37: System.out.print("try back later");
38: } catch (ExhibitClosed e) {// second catch block
39: System.out.print("not today");
40: }
41: }
42: There are three possibilities for when this code is run. If seeAnimal() doesn’t throw an
43: exception, nothing is printed out. If the animal is out for a walk, only the fi rst catch block
44: runs. If the exhibit is closed, only the second catch block runs.
45: A rule exists for the order of the catch blocks. Java looks at them in the order they
46: appear. If it is impossible for one of the catch blocks to be executed, a compiler error
47: about unreachable code occurs. This happens when a superclass is caught before a subclass.
48: Remember, we warned you to pay attention to any subclass exceptions.
49: In the porcupine example, the order of the catch blocks could be reversed because the
50: exceptions don’t inherit from each other. And yes, we have seen a porcupine be taken for a
51: walk on a leash.
52: The following example shows exception types that do inherit from each other:
53: public void visitMonkeys() {
54: try {
55: seeAnimal();
56: } catch (ExhibitClosedForLunch e) {// subclass exception
57: System.out.print("try back later");
58: } catch (ExhibitClosed e) {// superclass exception
59: System.out.print("not today");
60: }
61: }
```
If the more specifi c ExhibitClosedForLunch exception is thrown, the fi rst catch
block runs. If not, Java checks if the superclass ExhibitClosed exception is thrown
and catches it. This time, the order of the catch blocks does matter. The reverse does
not work.
```java
1: public void visitMonkeys() {
2: try {
3: seeAnimal();
4: } catch (ExhibitClosed e) {
5: System.out.print("not today");
6: } catch (ExhibitClosedForLunch e) {// DOES NOT COMPILE
7: System.out.print("try back later");
8: }
9: }
```
This time, if the more specific ExhibitClosedForLunch exception is thrown, the catch
block for ExhibitClosed runs—which means there is no way for the second catch block to
ever run. Java correctly tells us there is an unreachable catch block.

```java
13: catch block takes care of it, making sure there no way to get to the second catch block.
14: To review catching multiple exceptions, remember that at most one catch block will run
15: and it will be the fi rst catch block that can handle it.
16: Throwing a Second Exception
17: So far, we’ve limited ourselves to one try statement in each example. However,
18: a catch or finally block can have any valid Java code in it—including another
19: try statement.
20: may ask you about exception handling with those classes. This is actually a
21: basic Java syntax or exception handling!
22: The following code tries to read a file:
23: public static void main(String[] args) {
24:   FileReader reader = null;
25:   try {
26:     reader = read();
27:   } catch (IOException e) {
28:     try {
29:       if (reader != null) reader.close();
30:     } catch (IOException inner) {
31:     }
32:   }
33: }
34: private static FileReader read() throws IOException {

36: }
```

The easiest case is if line 28 doesn’t throw an exception. Then the entire catch block on
lines 20–25 is skipped. Next, consider if line 28 throws a NullPointerException. That isn’t
an IOException, so the catch block on lines 20–25 will still be skipped.
If line 28 does throw an IOException, the catch block on lines 20–25 does get run. Line
22 tries to close the reader. If that goes well, the code completes and the main() method
ends normally. If the close() method does throw an exception, Java looks for more catch
blocks. There aren’t any, so the main method throws that new exception. Regardless, the
exception on line 28 is handled. A different exception might be thrown, but the one from
line 28 is done.
Most of the examples you see with exception handling on the exam are abstract. They
use letters or numbers to make sure you understand the flow. This one shows that only the
last exception to be thrown matters. (This is true for the OCA exam. It will change a bit on
the OCP exam.)
```java
 1: try {
 2:   throw new RuntimeException();
 3: } catch (RuntimeException e) {
 4:   throw new RuntimeException();
 5: } finally {
 6:   throw new Exception();
 7: }
 8: Line 27 throws an exception, which is caught on line 28. The catch block then throws
 9: an exception on line 29. If there were no finally block, the exception from line 29 would
10: be thrown. However, the finally block runs after the try block. Since the finally block
11: throws an exception of its own on line 31, this one gets thrown. The exception from the
12: catch block gets forgotten about. This is why you often see another try/catch inside a
13: finally block—to make sure it doesn’t mask the exception from the catch block.
14: Next we are going to show you the hardest example you can be asked related to
15: exceptions. What do you think this method returns? Go slowly. It’s tricky.
16: public String exceptions() {
17:   String result = "";
18:   String v = null;
19:   try {
20:      try {
21:         result += "before";
22:         v.length();
23:         result += "after";
24:      } catch (NullPointerException e) {
25:        result += "catch";
26:        throw new RuntimeException();
27:      } finally {
28:        result += "finally";
29:        throw new Exception();
30:      }
31:   } catch (Exception e) {
32:     result += "done";
33:   }
34:   return result;
35: }
```

The correct answer is before catch finally done. Everything is normal up until line
35, when "before" is added. Line 36 throws a NullPointerException. Line 37 is skipped
as Java goes straight to the catch block. Line 38 does catch the exception, and "catch" is
added on line 39. Then line 40 throws a RuntimeException. The finally block runs after
the catch regardless of whether an exception is thrown; it adds "finally" to result. At this
point, we have completed the inner try statement that ran on lines 34–44. The outer catch
block then sees an exception was thrown and catches it on line 45; it adds "done" to result.
Recognizing Common Exception Types
You need to recognize three types of exceptions for the OCA exam: runtime exceptions,
checked exceptions, and errors. We’ll look at common examples of each type. For the
exam, you’ll need to recognize which type of an exception it is and whether it’s thrown by
the JVM or a programmer. So you can recognize them, we’ll show you some code examples
for those exceptions.
Runtime Exceptions
Runtime exceptions extend RuntimeException. They don’t have to be handled or declared.
They can be thrown by the programmer or by the JVM. Common runtime exceptions
include the following:
ArithmeticException             Thrown by the JVM when code attempts to divide by zero
ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException Thrown by the JVM when code uses an illegal
index to access an array
ClassCastException Thrown by the JVM when an attempt is made to cast an excep-
tion to a subclass of which it is not an instance
IllegalArgumentException Thrown by the programmer to indicate that a method has
been passed an illegal or inappropriate argument
NullPointerException Thrown by the JVM when there is a null reference where an
object is required
NumberFormatException Thrown by the programmer when an attempt is made to con-
vert a string to a numeric type but the string doesn’t have an appropriate format
ArithmeticException
Trying to divide an int by zero gives an undefi ned result. When this occurs, the JVM will
throw an ArithmeticException:
```java
 1: int answer = 11 / 0;
 2: Running this code results in the following output:
 3: Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
 4: Java doesn’t spell out the word “divide.” That’s okay, though, because we know that / is
 5: the division operator and that Java is trying to tell us division by zero occurred.
 6: The thread "main" is telling us the code was called directly or indirectly from a program
 7: with a main method. On the OCA exam, this is all the output we will see. Next comes the
 8: name of the exception, followed by extra information (if any) that goes with the exception.
 9: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
10: You know by now that array indexes start with 0 and go up to 1 less than the length of the
11: array—which means this code will throw an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException:
12: int[] countsOfMoose = new int[3];
13: System.out.println(countsOfMoose[-1]);
14: This is a problem because there’s no such thing as a negative array index. Running this
15: code yields the following output:
16: Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: -1
17: At least Java tells us what index was invalid. Can you see what’s wrong with this one?
18: int total = 0;
19: int[] countsOfMoose = new int[3];
20: for (int i = 0; i <= countsOfMoose.length; i++)
21: total += countsOfMoose[i];
22: The problem is that the for loop should have < instead of <=. On the fi nal iteration of
23: the loop, Java tries to call countsOfMoose[3], which is invalid. The array includes only
24: three elements, making 2 the largest possible index. The output looks like this:
25: Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: 3
26: ClassCastException
27: Java tries to protect you from impossible casts. This code doesn’t compile because Integer
28: is not a subclass of String:
29: String type = "moose";
30: Integer number = (Integer) type;       // DOES NOT COMPILE
31: More complicated code thwarts Java’s attempts to protect you. When the cast fails at
32: runtime, Java will throw a ClassCastException:
33: String type = "moose";
34: Object obj = type;
35: Integer number = (Integer) obj;
36: The compiler sees a cast from Object to Integer. This could be okay. The compiler
37: doesn’t realize there’s a String in that Object. When the code runs, it yields the following
38: output:
39: Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ClassCastException: java.lang.String
40: cannot be cast to java.lang.Integer
41: Java tells us both types that were involved in the problem, making it apparent what’s
42: wrong.
43: IllegalArgumentException
44: IllegalArgumentException is a way for your program to protect itself. We first saw the
45: following setter method in the Swan class in Chapter 4, “Methods and Encapsulation.”
46:   public void setNumberEggs(int numberEggs) {// setter
47:     if (numberEggs >= 0) // guard condition
48:       this.numberEggs = numberEggs;
49:   }
```
This code works, but we don’t really want to ignore the caller’s request when they tell
us a Swan has –2 eggs. We want to tell the caller that something is wrong—preferably in a
very obvious way that the caller can’t ignore so that the programmer will fi x the problem.
Exceptions are an efficient way to do this. Seeing the code end with an exception is a great
reminder that something is wrong:
```java
1: public static void setNumberEggs(int numberEggs) {
```
if (numberEggs < 0)
throw new IllegalArgumentException(
"# eggs must not be negative");
this.numberEggs = numberEggs;
```java
1: }
```
The program throws an exception when it’s not happy with the parameter values. The
output looks like this:
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: # eggs must not
be negative
Clearly this is a problem that must be fi xed if the programmer wants the program to do
anything useful.
NullPointerException
Instance variables and methods must be called on a non-null reference. If the reference is
null, the JVM will throw a NullPointerException. It’s usually subtle, such as this exam-
ple, which checks whether you remember instance variable references default to null.
String name;
```java
 1: public void printLength() throws NullPointerException {
 2: System.out.println(name.length());
 3: }
 4: Running this code results in this output:
 5: Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NullPointerException
 6: NumberFormatException
 7: Java provides methods to convert strings to numbers. When these are passed
 8: an invalid value, they throw a NumberFormatException. The idea is similar to
 9: IllegalArgumentException. Since this is a common problem, Java gives it a separate class.
10: In fact, NumberFormatException is a subclass of IllegalArgumentException. Here’s an
11: example of trying to convert something non-numeric into an int:
12: Integer.parseInt("abc");
13: The output looks like this:
14: Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NumberFormatException: For input string:
15: "abc"
16: Checked Exceptions
17: Checked exceptions have Exception in their hierarchy but not RuntimeException. They
18: must be handled or declared. They can be thrown by the programmer or by the JVM.
19: Common runtime exceptions include the following:
20: FileNotFoundException Thrown programmatically when code tries to reference a fi le
21: that does not exist
22: IOException Thrown programmatically when there’s a problem reading or writing a fi le
23: For the OCA exam, you only need to know that these are checked exceptions. Also keep
24: in mind that FileNotFoundException is a subclass of IOException, although the exam will
25: remind you of that fact if it comes up. You’ll see these two exceptions in more detail on the
26: OCP exam.
27: Errors
28: Errors extend the Error class. They are thrown by the JVM and should not be handled or
29: declared. Errors are rare, but you might see these:
30: ExceptionInInitializerError Thrown by the JVM when a static initializer throws
31: an exception and doesn’t handle it
32: StackOverflowError Thrown by the JVM when a method calls itself too many times
33: (this is called infinite recursion because the method typically calls itself without end)
34: NoClassDefFoundError Thrown by the JVM when a class that the code uses is available
35: at compile time but not runtime
36: ExceptionInInitializerError
37: Java runs static initializers the fi rst time a class is used. If one of the static initializers
38: throws an exception, Java can’t start using the class. It declares defeat by throwing an
39: ExceptionInInitializerError. This code shows an ArrayIndexOutOfBounds in a static
40: initializer:
41: static {
42: int[] countsOfMoose = new int[3];
43: int num = countsOfMoose[-1];
44: }
45: public static void main(String[] args) { }
```
This code yields information about two exceptions:
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ExceptionInInitializerError
Caused by: java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: -1
We get the ExceptionInInitializerError because the error happened in a static initial-
izer. That information alone wouldn’t be particularly useful in fixing the problem. Therefore,
Java also tells us the original cause of the problem: the ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException that
we need to fix.
The ExceptionInInitializerError is an error because Java failed to load the whole
class. This failure prevents Java from continuing.
StackOverflowError
When Java calls methods, it puts parameters and local variables on the stack. After doing
this a very large number of times, the stack runs out of room and overflows. This is called a
StackOverflowError. Most of the time, this error occurs when a method calls itself.
```java
1: public static void doNotCodeThis(int num) {
2: doNotCodeThis(1);
3: }
```
The output contains this line:
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.StackOverflowError
Since the method calls itself, it will never end. Eventually, Java runs out of room on the
stack and throws the error. This is called infi nite recursion. It is better than an infi nite loop
because at least Java will catch it and throw the error. With an infi nite loop, Java just uses
all your CPU until you can kill it.
NoClassDefFoundError
This error won’t show up in code on the exam—you just need to know that it is an error.
NoClassDefFoundError occurs when Java can’t fi nd the class at runtime.
Calling Methods That Throw Exceptions
When you’re calling a method that throws an exception, the rules are the same as within a
method. Do you see why the following doesn’t compile?
class NoMoreCarrotsException extends Exception {}
```java
1: public class Bunny {
2: public static void main(String[] args) {
3: eatCarrot();// DOES NOT COMPILE
4: }
5: private static void eatCarrot() throws NoMoreCarrotsException {
6: }
7: }
```
The problem is that NoMoreCarrotsException is a checked exception. Checked excep-
tions must be handled or declared. The code would compile if we changed the main()
method to either of these:
public static void main(String[] args)
throws NoMoreCarrotsException {// declare exception
```java
 1: eatCarrot();
 2: }
 3: public static void main(String[] args) {
 4: try {
 5: eatCarrot();
 6: } catch (NoMoreCarrotsException e ) {// handle exception
 7: System.out.print("sad rabbit");
 8: }
 9: }
10: You might have noticed that eatCarrot() didn’t actually throw an exception; it just
11: declared that it could. This is enough for the compiler to require the caller to handle or
12: declare the exception.
13: The compiler is still on the lookout for unreachable code. Declaring an unused exception
14: isn’t considered unreachable code. It gives the method the option to change the implementa-
15: tion to throw that exception in the future. Do you see the issue here?
16: public void bad() {
17: try {
18: eatCarrot();
19: } catch (NoMoreCarrotsException e ) {// DOES NOT COMPILE
20: System.out.print("sad rabbit");
21: }
22: }
23: public void good() throws NoMoreCarrotsException {
24: eatCarrot();
25: }
26: private static void eatCarrot() { }
27: Java knows that eatCarrot() can’t throw a checked exception—which means there’s no
28: way for the catch block in bad() to be reached. In comparison, good() is free to declare
29: other exceptions.
30: Subclasses
31: Now that you have a deeper understanding of exceptions, let’s look at overriding meth-
32: ods with exceptions in the method declaration. When a class overrides a method from a
33: superclass or implements a method from an interface, it’s not allowed to add new checked
34: exceptions to the method signature. For example, this code isn’t allowed:
35: class CanNotHopException extends Exception { }
36: class Hopper {
37: public void hop() { }
38: }
39: class Bunny extends Hopper {
40: public void hop() throws CanNotHopException { } // DOES NOT COMPILE
41: }
42: Java knows hop() isn’t allowed to throw any checked exceptions because the superclass
43: Hopper doesn’t declare any. Imagine what would happen if subclasses could add checked
44: exceptions—you could write code that calls Hopper’s hop() method and not handle any
45: exceptions. Then if Bunny was used in its place, the code wouldn’t know to handle or
46: declare CanNotHopException.
47: A subclass is allowed to declare fewer exceptions than the superclass or interface. This is
48: legal because callers are already handling them.
49: class Hopper {
50: public void hop() throws CanNotHopException { }
51: }
52: class Bunny extends Hopper {
53: public void hop() { }
54: }
55: A subclass not declaring an exception is similar to a method declaring it throws an
56: exception that it never actually throws. This is perfectly legal.
57: Similarly, a class is allowed to declare a subclass of an exception type. The idea is the
58: same. The superclass or interface has already taken care of a broader type. Here’s an
59: example:
60: class Hopper {
61: public void hop() throws Exception { }
62: }
63: class Bunny extends Hopper {
64: public void hop() throws CanNotHopException { }
65: }
66: Bunny could declare that it throws Exception directly, or it could declare that it throws a
67: more specific type of Exception. It could even declare that it throws nothing at all.
68: This rule applies only to checked exceptions. The following code is legal because it has a
69: runtime exception in the subclass’s version:
70: class Hopper {
71: public void hop() { }
72: }
73: class Bunny extends Hopper {
74: public void hop() throws IllegalStateException { }
75: }
```
The reason that it’s okay to declare new runtime exceptions in a subclass method is that
the declaration is redundant. Methods are free to throw any runtime exceptions they want
without mentioning them in the method declaration.
Printing an Exception
There are three ways to print an exception. You can let Java print it out, print just the mes-
sage, or print where the stack trace comes from. This example shows all three approaches:
```java
 1: public static void main(String[] args) {
 2:    try {
 3:     hop();
 4:    } catch (Exception e) {
 5:      System.out.println(e);
 6:     System.out.println(e.getMessage());
 7:     e.printStackTrace();
 8:   }
 9: }
10: private static void hop() {
11:   throw new RuntimeException("cannot hop");
12: }
```
This code results in the following output:
java.lang.RuntimeException: cannot hop
cannot hop
java.lang.RuntimeException: cannot hop
at trycatch.Handling.hop(Handling.java:15)
at trycatch.Handling.main(Handling.java:7)
The fi rst line shows what Java prints out by default: the exception type and message. The
second line shows just the message. The rest shows a stack trace.
The stack trace is usually the most helpful one because it shows where the exception
occurred in each method that it passed through. On the OCA exam, you will mostly see the
fi rst approach. This is because the exam often shows code snippets.
The stack trace shows all the methods on the stack. Figure 6.4 shows what the stack
looks like for this code. Every time you call a method, Java adds it to the stack until it com-
pletes. When an exception is thrown, it goes through the stack until it fi nds a method that
can handle it or it runs out of stack.
FIGURE 6.4          A method stack
Why Swallowing Exception Is Bad
Because checked exceptions require you to handle or declare them, there is a temptation
to catch them so they “go away.” But doing so can cause problems. In the following code,
there’s a problem reading in the ﬁle:
```java
 1: public static void main(String[] args) {
 2: String textInFile = null;
 3: try {
 4:   readInFile();
 5: } catch (IOException e) {
 6: // ignore exception
 7: }
 8: // imagine many lines of code here
 9: System.out.println(textInFile.replace(" ", ""));
10: }
11: private static void readInFile() throws IOException {
12: throw new IOException();
13: }
```
The code results in a NullPointerException. Java doesn’t tell you anything about the
original IOException because it was handled. Granted, it was handled poorly, but it was
handled.
When writing your own code, print out a stack trace or at least a message when catching
an exception. Also, consider whether continuing is the best course of action. In our exam-
ple, the program can’t do anything after it fails to read in the ﬁle. It might as well have just
thrown the IOException.
Summary
An exception indicates something unexpected happened. A method can handle an excep-
tion by catching it or declaring it for the caller to deal with. Many exceptions are thrown
by Java libraries. You can throw your own exception with code such as throw new
Exception().
Subclasses of java.lang.Error are exceptions that a programmer should not attempt to
handle. Subclasses of java.lang.RuntimeException are runtime (unchecked) exceptions.
Subclasses of java.lang.Exception, but not java.lang.RuntimeException are checked
exceptions. Java requires checked exceptions to be handled or declared.
If a try statement has multiple catch blocks, at most one catch block can run. Java
looks for an exception that can be caught by each catch block in the order they appear, and
the fi rst match is run. Then execution continues after the try statement. If both catch and
finally throw an exception, the one from finally gets thrown.
Common runtime exceptions include:
■    ArithmeticException
■    ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
■    ClassCastException
■    IllegalArgumentException
■    NullPointerException
■    NumberFormatException
IllegalArgumentException and NumberFormatException are typically thrown by the
programmer, whereas the others are typically thrown by the JVM.
Common checked exceptions include:
■    IOException
■    FileNotFoundException
Common errors include:
■    ExceptionInInitializerError
■    StackOverflowError
■    NoClassDefFoundError
When a method overrides a method in a superclass or interface, it is not allowed to add
checked exceptions. It is allowed to declare fewer exceptions or declare a subclass of a
declared exception. Methods declare exceptions with the keyword throws.
Exam Essentials
Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions. Unchecked exceptions are also
known as runtime exceptions and are subclasses of java.lang.RuntimeException. All
other subclasses of java.lang.Exception are checked exceptions.
Understand the flow of a try statement. A try statement must have a catch or a finally
block. Multiple catch blocks are also allowed, provided no superclass exception type
appears in an earlier catch block than its subclass. The finally block runs last regardless
of whether an exception is thrown.
Identify whether an exception is thrown by the programmer or the JVM. Illegal
ArgumentException and NumberFormatException are commonly thrown by the program-
mer. Most of the other runtime exceptions are typically thrown by the JVM.
Declare methods that declare exceptions. The throws keyword is used in a method decla-
ration to indicate an exception might be thrown. When overriding a method, the method is
allowed to throw fewer exceptions than the original version.
Recognize when to use throw versus throws. The throw keyword is used when you actu-
ally want to throw an exception—for example, throw new RuntimeException(). The
throws keyword is used in a method declaration.
